svg.text: MISHNAIC HEBREW
SCRIPT — RABBINIC WRITING
MISHNAIC PERIOD SCRIPT (~200-500 CE)
Same square Assyrian block script as Biblical Hebrew
Mishnah (c. 200 CE): compiled by Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi in Hebrew
Talmud (c. 500 CE): mostly Aramaic; Hebrew for Mishnah quotations
RASHI SCRIPT (SEMI-CURSIVE)
Rashi script: semi-cursive style used for rabbinic commentary
Named for Rashi (1040-1105 CE); actually Sephardic cursive script
Talmud editions: main text in square script, commentary in Rashi script
Readers of Talmud must know both script styles simultaneously
MISHNAIC VOCABULARY IN SCRIPT
New words appear in Mishnaic period: Aramaic loans, Greek loans
Aramaic: sanhedrin, mamzer, get (legal divorce document)
Greek: prozbul, sanhedrin (same word in both — via Aramaic)
SAMPLE MISHNAIC TEXT
Eilu devarim she-ein lahem shiur...
"These are things that have no measure..." (Mishnah Peah 1:1)
ARABIC · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ARABIC SCRIPT IN THE RABBINIC PERIOD
Early Arabic inscriptions: Safaitic (~2nd century BCE-4th CE)
Nabataean script → Arabic script: transition 4th-6th century CE
Quran (7th century CE): crystallized Arabic script as we know it
Hebrew and Arabic scripts both mature during same centuries
ARABIC IN JEWISH SCHOLARSHIP
Judeo-Arabic: Hebrew letters to write Arabic (like Yiddish with Hebrew)
Maimonides (Rambam): wrote Guide for the Perplexed in Judeo-Arabic
Sa'adia Gaon: translated Torah into Arabic (Tafsir, ~930 CE)
SCRIPT INTERACTION
Both scripts: consonantal abjad, right-to-left, vowel points optional
Jewish scholars in Islamic lands read Hebrew script daily + Arabic script
Judeo-Arabic = Arabic language in Hebrew letters = bilingual script life
PLATE 10a ALT · MISHNAIC HEBREW / ARABIC · SCRIPT
